title: BIG SUPER IMPROVES ADVICE
full_text:
The $4.1 trillion Australian superannuation industry, led by AustralianSuper, UniSuper and others, is finally doing its job and improving its retirement credentials.
Industry behemoth AustralianSuper this week confirmed it has followed UniSuper’s move last year in signing omni-channel technology advice provider Ignition Advice.
The aim is to grab the attention of the so-called missing middle – the millions of Australians who cannot afford or do not see the need for financial advice.
Ignition is rare in the financial technology space targeted at the fund level, offering members and advisers access while helping to streamline the process for everyone.
It works by signing the fund to five-plus-year deals after 12 months or more of due diligence working with the funds and advisers such as locally based Finura Group.
Signing AustralianSuper was a coup for Ignition, which is working to build momentum ahead of a potential public listing.
There is an element of defence involved behind the industry funds’ moves, needing to work with advisers in an attempt to slow the steady leaking of older customers as independent advisers take members to platforms such as HUB24.
AustralianSuper’s retirement boss, Shane Hancock, put it differently in a statement, saying: “We want all members to feel empowered, informed and excited about their retirements. Getting quality financial advice and guidance makes a real difference to member confidence.” The federal government is still sitting on necessary decisions on legislative changes clarifying just what levels of advice funds can give.
But APRA and others have pushed the industry to wake up to its obligation to support members in retirement.
The superannuation industry’s change from an accumulation fund to a payments vehicle brings on a whole new set of responsibilities, including cyber fraud and, of course, advice.
The move to efficiently scale advice delivery through technology providers such as Ignition is a welcome step in the right direction.
The funds are also stepping up member education but, with the exception of the likes of UniSuper, ART and Aware, the industry is lagging on new product delivery.
UniSuper is still in a trial phase with its Ignition platform, with 10,000 members starting digital consultation and 3500 receiving formal advice.
The recipients range right across its member age bracket.
Ignition’s own journey, in some respects, mirrors the progress Australian industry funds have en masse made on the way to member retirements.
The Sydney-based company started life as a “digital calculator” or robo-advice house established by Mark Fordree and Michael Giles in 2014.
At the time, digital advice platforms were simply computer algorithms that worked out, based on how much you had to invest and your risk tolerance, what you should invest in.
The target market was financial advisers who directed clients to their favourite channels.
An early Ignition backer was Count Financial founder Barry Lambert. He made his name selling Count to CBA for $373m in 2011 and, post-royal commission, his old company bought it back for a few million dollars – once again proving the late Kerry Packer wrong, showing that there are indeed many more Alan Bonds in the market.
Lambert invested $1m in Ignition for a 20 per cent stake last decade as it was heading offshore.
Legislative changes in the UK proved a game changer and, in 2017, the company went to Europe looking for clients, spearheaded by new recruit and present boss Terry Donohoe.
The 2021 deal with Bank of Ireland was the launch pad, and clients now run up to Spanish banking giant Santander.
The UK legislative changes helped open the door to financial digital acceptance and new financial delivery vehicles like Ignition.
Australian governments on both sides of the fence have talked a good game but dithered on key reforms, such as expanding the scope of advice that super funds can give and refining the best interest duty provisions.
Financial Services Minister Dan Mulino wants to clear the fallout from the Shield and First Guardian Master Fund collapses before finalising the long-sought-after advice reforms.
He does not seem to realise just maybe a better advice regime, giving super funds more authority, may help prevent the inevitable further collapses and assist in the compensation.
Granted, the Australian financial sector has not exactly shot the lights out when it comes to earning customer trust, so government caution is not surprising.
The omni-channel technology offered by Ignition is aimed at helping funds scale advice by opening the door to members, advisers and administrators.
The offering is a self-service technology that members can use to choose the best investment offer with the option of calling in personal advice if necessary.
Members have the best of both worlds, while the fund can serve more people faster and in the process maintain member confidence and loyalty.
AustralianSuper may have lagged UniSuper by 12 months or more, but its sheer scale is another wake-up call for the rest of the industry.
Members can work on their fund investments in their own time, on their own, with the help of advisers or in a hybrid model.
Even after adding AustralianSuper to the list, Ignition’s local revenue is only 35 per cent of the 80-person company’s total revenue as it heads back home to fill in the gaps left by a lack of local demand.
It has now signed up two of the top four funds and more are soon to follow. The Dublin-based Donohoe now spends around one-third of his time in Australia and the rest in London.
Ignition has been on the Industry Fund Services books for many years, but with the help of consultants such as Finura Group the due diligence process on technology overhauls can take 12 months or more.
Donohoe says the product is designed for “cost-effective scalable omni-channel digital advice and guidance models that offer seamless interoperability, are extremely flexible and, critically, are assured from a conduct risk perspective”.
His pitch says that the platforms deliver an 85 per cent reduction in the elapsed time to deliver regulated advice, adviser productivity increasing by a factor of 15 times and overheads associated with advice provision reducing by 60 per cent.
AI is, of course, an integral part of the platform and Ignition works with Anthropic’s Claude chatbot to cut delivery times further.
A key benefit is Ignition provides the platform for the fund to develop and deliver on its own brand to its customers.
Built for success Wesfarmers is trading at a premium to the market, at about 30 times earnings against 21 times.
To maintain that outperformance, it will take Bunnings to match Kmart’s recent record, growing earnings at 7 per cent on 3.3 per cent revenue growth.
Bunnings’ first half showed a creditable 5 per cent earnings lift on revenue growth of 4.2 per cent, but the market wants more.
The $100m Built Living joint venture, using advanced manufacturing to build homes cheaper and faster, will not shift the Wesfarmers earnings dial any time soon.
But it ticks a lot of boxes for CEO Rob Scott.
The factory north of Perth is on land won in a state government ballot with guaranteed state support; the venture is aimed at helping to solve the national housing shortage and, in time, may be a profitable venture on Wesfarmers’ return on capital benchmark.
Kerry Stokes had equal cross-over interest with his Boral unit, but Wesfarmers took the local politically attractive prize.
The Built modular construction product is shown to deliver homes and apartments at 20 per cent lower costs and 50 per cent faster in a model based on building pre-fab blocks.
No, it will not be bigger than Bunnings any time soon, but it is a home-grown customer in a high-growth market and, while time will tell whether it meets the return hurdle, a conglomerate needs to continue diversification. Outgoing chair Michael Chaney will not be available for carpentry work on the modular homes.
summary: AustralianSuper has partnered with Ignition Advice to enhance financial advice accessibility for members, particularly targeting those who typically do not seek financial guidance.

heading: Superannuation and Investments
key_points: The partnership aims to engage the 'missing middle' of Australians who may not afford or see the need for financial advice.
Ignition Advice provides a self-service technology platform that allows members to access investment options and personal advice as needed.
The superannuation industry is shifting from accumulation to payment vehicles, increasing the need for member education and advice.
relevance: This development highlights the evolving landscape of financial advice in superannuation, which is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals involved in retirement planning and member support.
